chunk_summary: Environmental enteric dysfunction (EED) is a chronic subclinical condition impairing growth via gut inflammation and systemic effects. It is not a routine diagnosis but requires addressing diet, WASH behaviors, and early diarrhea treatment while ruling out other treatable conditions.
learning_objectives: Understand the pathophysiology of environmental enteric dysfunction (EED) and its impact on growth.
Identify key interventions to mitigate EED, including dietary improvements and WASH practices.
Recognize when to evaluate for alternative diagnoses in cases of persistent symptoms or growth failure.
key_facts: EED is driven by repeated fecal–oral pathogen exposure, leading to gut inflammation and systemic effects like reduced IGF-1 activity.
EED is not a routine bedside diagnosis; biomarkers like stool inflammatory markers and lactulose:mannitol tests are not standardized for clinical use.
Management focuses on improving diet quality, protein adequacy, WASH behaviors, and early diarrhea treatment with ORS and zinc.
Persistent diarrhea, malabsorption, or systemic symptoms should prompt evaluation for coeliac disease, giardiasis, TB, or inflammatory bowel disease.
Growth monitoring over 8–12 weeks is essential to assess intervention effectiveness.
reference_quote: The WASH Benefits and SHINE trials: interpretation and implications for policy and practice — https://www.thelancet.com/journals/langlo/article/PIIS2
document_key_topics: Differential diagnosis in chronic gut conditions
